coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission red carbon dots for ATP real-time monitoring and quantification to reveal drug and cancer effects on lysosomes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jing
Wang, Tianmiao
Xu, Jingyuan
Hai, Jun
Ji, Yibing
Li, Ruijun
coredata.dc:description: Monitoring and quantifying ATP levels in vivo is essential to understanding its role as a signaling molecule in tumor progression and therapy. Nevertheless, the real-time monitoring and quantitative assessment of lysosomal ATP remains challenging due to the lack of accurate tools in deep tissues. In this study, based on the crosslinking enhanced emission (CEE) effect, we successfully synthesized red carbon dots (R-CDs) with dual emission properties for efficient quantification of intracellular ATP. The R-CDs emit in the near-infrared range and target lysosomes with rapid detection capabilities, rendering them exceptionally well-suited for directly observing and analyzing the dynamics of lysosomal ATP through live cell imaging techniques. Importantly, R-CDs have proven their efficacy in real-time monitoring of drug stimulus-induced fluctuations in endogenous lysosomal ATP concentration and have also been employed for quantifying and distinguishing lysosomal ATP levels among normal and cancer cell lines. These noteworthy findings emphasize the versatility of the R-CD as a valuable imaging tool for elucidating the functional role of lysosomal ATP in drug screening and cancer diagnostics and hold the promise of becoming a reference tool for deepening our understanding of drug mechanisms of action.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adenosine triphosphate
Ratio fluorescence
Lysosomes
Real-time monitoring
Quantification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy for R-CDs
Analytical performance of R-CDs for ATP
Application of the R-CDs for ATP in human serum
Colocalization experiment with lysosomes in cells
Real-time monitoring of fluctuations in endogenous lysosomal ATP levels
Quantification of lysosome ATP concentration in different cell lines

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM images of R-CDs with variations in size. (b) FTIR of OPD, SA, NR and R-CD. High-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) O 1s, and (f) P 2p.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs with different concentrations of ATP. (b) Standard curve of the fluorescence of R-CDs dependent on the concentration of ATP. (c) The specific selection of R-CDs for common intracellular substances, common metal ions and analogues. 1–22: Blank, ascorbic acid, Ca2+, Cu2+, dopamine, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Na+, proline, phenylalanine, glutathione, urea, tryptophan, aspartic acid, histidine, UTP、CTP、GTP、AMP、 ADP and ATP. (d) The zeta potential of R-CDs, ATP, and R-CDs containing ATP. (e) Decline curve of fluorescence lifetime of R-CD, ATP-containing (0.5 mM) R-CD at 560 nm. (f) Decline curve of fluorescence lifetime of R-CD, ATP-containing (0.5 mM) R-CD at 625 nm. (g) Possible mechanism of ATP response by R-CDs.

(a) Fluorescent images of LO2 cells after 1 h of staining with CD stain. (b) Fluorescence images of cells stained with LTG (50 nM) for 30 min. (c) Bright field, (d) Combined images of panels (a), (b), and (c). (e) FI curves of the relevant regions (white line in d). Scale bar, 20 μm.

(a) Fluorescence images after incubating LO2 cells with R-CDs for 1 h and stimulating with 10 μM chloroquine every 2 min. (b) The normalized two-channel fluorescence intensity ratio (Iyellow/Ired) was obtained from the LO2 cells in panel (a). (c) Fluorescence images of LO2 cells were obtained after 1 h incubation with R-CDs followed by stimulation with 50 μM etoposide every 2 min. (d) The normalized two-channel fluorescence intensity ratio (Iyellow/Ired) was obtained from LO2 cells in panel (c). (a) Fluorescence images of LO2 cells cultured with R-CDs, glucose-free medium and R-CDs, etoposide and R-CDs, chloroquine and R-CDs, and APAP and R-CDs. (b) Normalized fluorescence intensity ratios (Iyellow/Ired) were obtained for the five conditions. Scale bar, 20 μm in (a) (c), 5 μm in (e).

Quantifying Lysosomal ATP in Different Cell Lines with R-CDs. (a) Confocal microscopy images. (b) ATP concentrations in various cell lines, presented as mean ± standard deviation (s.d.) values (mM) with 'n' denoting cell count. Scale bar: 10/20 μm.
Illustration of R-CDs and application to the in vitro detection of intracellular lysosomal ATP level fluctuations.

head.ce:title: Dual-emission red carbon dots for ATP real-time monitoring and quantification to reveal drug and cancer effects on lysosomes

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Analytical Chemistry, China Pharmaceutical University, 24 TongJiaXiang, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 210009, China.
Corresponding author. Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou, 730000, China.
Corresponding author. Department of Analytical Chemistry, China Pharmaceutical University, 24 TongJiaXiang, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 210009, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Monitoring and quantifying ATP levels in vivo is essential to understanding its role as a signaling molecule in tumor progression and therapy. Nevertheless, the real-time monitoring and quantitative assessment of lysosomal ATP remains challenging due to the lack of accurate tools in deep tissues. In this study, based on the crosslinking enhanced emission (CEE) effect, we successfully synthesized red carbon dots (R-CDs) with dual emission properties for efficient quantification of intracellular ATP. The R-CDs emit in the near-infrared range and target lysosomes with rapid detection capabilities, rendering them exceptionally well-suited for directly observing and analyzing the dynamics of lysosomal ATP through live cell imaging techniques. Importantly, R-CDs have proven their efficacy in real-time monitoring of drug stimulus-induced fluctuations in endogenous lysosomal ATP concentration and have also been employed for quantifying and distinguishing lysosomal ATP levels among normal and cancer cell lines. These noteworthy findings emphasize the versatility of the R-CD as a valuable imaging tool for elucidating the functional role of lysosomal ATP in drug screening and cancer diagnostics and hold the promise of becoming a reference tool for deepening our understanding of drug mechanisms of action.

In this work, R-CDs with dual emission were synthesized and successfully applied to real-time monitoring of lysosomal ATP dynamics in living cells, as well as assessing the lysosomal status of different cell lines and efficiently distinguishing between normal cells and cancer cells.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Among organelles, lysosomes play an important modulatory role in cellular perception, removal of waste products, and maintaining cellular homeostasis [1–3]. Impaired lysosomal function has been linked to various diseases, such as cancer, Parkinson's disease, and Alzheimer's disease [4,5]. Numerous studies have demonstrated that ATP is stored in the acidic lysosomal organelle, where it acts as a cytoplasmic agonist capable of altering lysosomal pH and inducing cytoplasmic deacidification [6,7]. Adenosine triphosphate (ATP), a basic biological anion, serves as a major source of energy for cellular processes [8,9]. It is an intra- or extracellular modeling factor involved in organ development, neurotransmission, and insulin secretion [10,11].ATP levels were reported to be abnormally higher in cancerous tissues than in normal tissues, as well as disruption of ATP homeostasis is associated with autophagy [12–14]. Furthermore, various drugs, particularly anticancer drugs, can induce changes in ATP concentrations in lysosomes [15,16]. Consequently, accurate detection and timely measurement of lysosomal ATP levels are essential for a comprehensive view of lysosomally relevant biological processes and to facilitate the selection of effective drugs.

To date, only a few ATP probes have been successfully employed for live cell lysosomal ATP imaging, and no suitable fluorescent probes exist for accurate quantification of lysosomal ATP concentration [17,18]. The majority of fluorescent probes used for ATP detection are small molecule single fluorescent probes and aptamers [19,20]. These probes not only required complex synthesis methods but were also susceptible to environmental factors that can compromise analysis accuracy [21,22]. Consequently, there is a critical necessity to develop a dual-emission fluoroprobe for precise measurement of lysosomal ATP levels. Previous studies have employed carbon dots (CDs) as fluorophores for ATP detection, but these methods often required the incorporation of metal ions or additional modifications with nucleic acid aptamers [23]. Furthermore, the use of these CDs was limited in its ability to directly detect ATP within cells because of their natural short-wavelength fluorescence and a restricted dynamic range of detection, thus diminishing their suitability for intracellular ATP monitoring. It is reassuring that the luminescent properties of CDs can be used to construct long-wavelength ratiometric fluorescent sensors by selecting suitable precursor molecules and forming covalent cross-linking frameworks during the synthesis process by using the cross-linking enhanced emission principle (CEE). Therefore, adding sodium alginate (SA) and phosphoric acid as cross-linking agents and neutral red (NR) as a red light source during the synthesis of CDs can effectively link adjacent oligomers during carbonation and polymerization, thus promoting the formation of covalently cross-linked structures [24,25].

In this study, we designed a dual-emission fluoroprobe to measure lysosomal ATP dynamics in viable cells (Scheme 1 ). Neutral red, sodium alginate, phosphoric acid, and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were used as precursors to synthesize N, P co-doped red CDs (R-CDs) by a one-step hydrothermal method and successfully constructed ratiometric fluorescent sensing due to the CEE effect. Specifically, the R-CDs achieved ATP detection within the lysosome, while their fluorescence remained unchanged when dispersed in other organelles, cytoplasm, or extracellularly. However, upon entry into the lysosomal environment and ATP binding, the red light of the R-CDs burst, while the yellow light exhibited minimal changes. On this basis, a rapid response of the fluorescent probe to ATP was observed upon exposure to certain drug stimuli, enabling real-time observation of dynamic changes within the lysosome and providing the possibility of drug screening. Furthermore, the fluorescence readings of intracellular R-CDs were positively correlated, facilitating the accurate construction of standard curves for intracellular ATP levels and the effective differentiation of lysosomal ATP levels in different cell lines for effective cancer diagnosis. Therefore, the construction of a self-calibrating fluorescent probe for rapid, sensitive, and accurate analysis of lysosomal ATP opens a new path for the design and development of dual-emission fluorescent sensors for disease diagnosis and drug screening.
For detailed descriptions of all materials and instruments, refer to Supporting Information.

R-CDs were synthesized by solvothermal method. SA (197 mg), OPD (216 mg), and NR (10 mg) were added to 27 ml of deionized water and heated until completely dissolved, followed by adding 3 ml of phosphoric acid and stirring the mixture until a homogeneous solution was formed, and then heated at 160 °C for 5 h. The final R-CDs solution was dialyzed with deionized water (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 24 h. CDs without SA or phosphate were synthesized in the same way.

After 100-fold diluting the R-CDs stock solution with PBS buffer (5 mM, pH = 7.4), different concentrations of ATP solution were added. 30 min later the UV–visible absorption spectra were measured in the 300–700 nm wavelength range, fluorescence emission spectra were obtained at 520–700 nm with an excitation wavelength of 505 nm.

All experiments were performed in live cells. Excitation wavelengths of 488 nm and 555 nm were selected, and acquisition wavelengths ranged from 490 to 570 nm (yellow channel) and 610–700 nm (red channel). Complete information is provided in the Supporting Information.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to examine the shape and size distribution of the R-CDs. The TEM results showed that the size distribution of R-CD is homogeneous with a mean size of 6.7 nm. The amorphous nature of the R-CDs was revealed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images, which displayed a lattice spacing of 0.18 nm. Interestingly, the R-CDs did not have a graphene structure, unlike graphene quantum dots.

To analyze the groups on the surface of the R-CDs, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was conducted, as shown in Fig. 1b. The FT-IR spectrum of SA exhibited absorption peaks at 3272 cm−1 (O–H stretching), 1616 cm−1 (CO stretching), and 1304 cm−1 (C–O in glycosidic bond). The FT-IR spectrum of neutral red displayed an N–H stretching vibration at approximately 3311 cm−1, along with aromatic structure stretching vibration peaks at 1627 cm−1 and 1502 cm−1. Analysis of the FT-IR spectrum of the R-CDs revealed an O–H and N–H stretching vibration peak at around 3415 cm−1, and a CO stretching vibration peak at approximately 1625 cm−1. Furthermore, characteristic absorption bands of PO (1460 cm−1) and P–O-R (1120 cm−1, where R represents an aromatic structure) were observed, confirming the successful doping of phosphorus (P) into the R-CDs during the hydrothermal reaction [26]. These results indicated the presence of functional groups, including amino and hydroxyl groups, as well as an aromatic ring structure within the R-CDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were also conducted to further analyze the elemental composition of R-CDs. The full-range XPS spectra (Fig. 1c–f and Fig. S1) revealed distinct peaks corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, P 2s, P 2p, and Na 1s. In the high-resolution C 1s spectrum, two peaks represented CC/CN (284.17 eV) and C–O/C–N (286.28 eV) bonds. The N 1s spectrum displayed two peaks indicating C–N/CN and N–H functionality, while the O 1s spectra represent C–O and PO/CO bonds. The P 2p XPS spectra revealed the presence of PO (134.58 eV) and PC/PN (135.58 eV) bonds. Moreover, the XPS results confirmed the presence of sodium (Na) and phosphorus (P) elements in the R-CDs, supporting the successful involvement of sodium alginate (SA) and phosphate in the R-CDs synthesis process. These findings were consistent with the FT-IR results, validating the presence of specific surface groups on the R-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy for R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Unlike traditional single-strength probes, ratiometric probes can mitigate the adverse impacts of experimental conditions by providing a favorable multi-channel fluorescence signal output [27]. To confirm whether SA and phosphoric acid cross-linking groups on the surface of CDs change the properties of CDs, R-CDs were respectively compared with synthetic CDs without SA or phosphoric acid. Under 350 nm excitation, R-CDs had a strongly emitting fluorescence peak around 450 nm (Fig. S2), whereas the fluorescence of CDs without SA or phosphoric acid was drastically reduced in large intensity here, suggesting that this emission peak was the combined effect of SA and phosphoric acid (Fig. S3). Under 505 nm excitation, R-CDs had emission peaks at 560 nm and 625 nm, while both other two CDs had only one emission peak, indicating that the involvement of SA and phosphoric acid promotes the formation of the covalent cross-linking structure during carbonation and polymerization to construct a dual-emission fluorescent probe due to the CEE effect. The covalent cross-linking restriction is likely to be formed at high temperatures, where CN groups are formed on the surface of the CDs, and these are phosphorescent emitters, which is why the emission peak at 450 nm is so strong [24]. Meanwhile, the emission peaks of CDs were significantly red-shifted with the involvement of phosphoric acid, indicating the role of heteroatom doping in the synthesis of red CDs [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of R-CDs for ATP
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the potential interaction between R-CDs and ATP, the solution of ATP (0.1 mM) was introduced to the R-CDs in PBS. As depicted in the corresponding Fig. 2 a, the R-CDs exhibited robust background fluorescence in the absence of ATP. Upon the addition of ATP, a noticeable reduction in the fluorescence peak at 625 nm was observed upon excitation at 505 nm, accompanied by a marginal enhancement in the emission peak at 560 nm. Subsequently, the analytical performance of the R-CDs, including linearity, the limit of detection (LOD), and selectivity, was evaluated under optimized conditions (pH = 7.4, incubated for 30 min). The fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs at 560 and 625 nm changed as ATP concentration increased. Notably, a well-defined linear correlation between the R-CDs and ATP concentration within the range of 2–100 μM was established (I560/I625 = 0.279 + 0.00262x, R2 = 0.998), with the limit of detection (LOD) of 1.04 μM (Fig. 2b). The analytical performance of R-CDs is better than the majority of previously reported works (Table S2). The concentration of ATP in the intracellular cytoplasm can be as high as 1–10 mM, whereas pretty low(<0.4 mM) in the extracellular environment [29]. Therefore, R-CD is sensitive enough to monitor intracellular ATP.

R-CDs is highly specific for ATP, and this was evaluated by detecting biological interferences. As shown in Fig. 2c, the obtained results substantiated the favorable selectivity of the R-CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor towards ATP. Fig. S5 illustrated that the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs underwent a mere 5 % change after continuous exposure to UV light for 1 h, highlighting the excellent photostability of the R-CDs + ATP system throughout this duration, which holds significant importance for prolonged monitoring purposes. Reaction time is another important factor in assessing the feasibility of real-time detection of fluorescent probes. Therefore, we evaluated the reaction time of R-CDs to ATP. The fluorescence at 625 nm was quenched immediately after adding 100 mM ATP (Fig. S6), and the reaction was almost completed within 2 min, which indicates that R-CDs have a rapid response to ATP.

It was postulated that the amino groups and quaternary ammonium cations present serve as a reactive site for ATP (Fig. 2g). As depicted in Fig. 2d, when ATP reacted with R-CDs, the zeta potential of R-CDs was observed to be decreased considerably to the weaker positive one, and this alteration implied the occurrence of electrostatic interactions between R-CDs and ATP. Thus, it is presumed that the initial binding of ATP to R-CDs is facilitated by the sec- and tert-amine groups on R-CDs through electrostatic interactions [17]. Particularly, ATP itself lacked fluorescence, and its absorption peak at 240 nm (Fig. S4a), which did not overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs, effectively ruled out the internal filtering effects (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) as the underlying mechanism. The absence of new emission peaks in the UV spectra of the R-CDs following the addition of ATP further excluded a static burst mechanism (Fig. S4b). In Fig. 2f, the fluorescence lifetime at 625 nm decreased from 3.79 ns to 1.93 ns after the addition of ATP solution (0.5 mM) to R-CDs. This finding indicates the involvement of a dynamic burst mechanism in the R-CDs' fluorescence response to ATP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the R-CDs for ATP in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the potential of R-CDs for practical applications, ATP concentrations were measured in two kinds of human serum samples from the Second Affiliated Hospital of Lanzhou University, which were diluted 50-fold with deionized water before the experiment. After adding 20 μL of serum samples with different dilutions, which contained different concentrations of ATP, to the aforementioned reaction system, the fluorescence spectra were carefully measured. As shown in Table S1, R-CDs were used to detect ATP in normal human serum samples, with recoveries ranging from 99.8 % to 106.1 % and RSDs below 3.7 %. For serum samples from individuals with abnormal liver function, the recoveries ranged from 94.4 % to 104.8 %, with an RSD of less than 3.9 %. These results suggest that R-CDs can be used to measure ATP in actual serum samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colocalization experiment with lysosomes in cells
ce:section.ce:para: MTT assays were performed to evaluate the biocompatibility of R-CDs, revealing no significant impact on cellular activity when R-CDs were diluted at a ratio of 10-fold and higher (Fig. S7). To examine the potential co-localization of R-CDs with lysosomes, Lyso-Tracker Green (LTG), a commercially available green fluorescent dye specific to lysosomes, was employed in the experimental investigations. Fig. 3 depicted cellular images stained with both R-CDs and the aforementioned commercial dyes. Remarkably, the red (representing R-CDs) and green (representing LTG) images exhibited a seamless fusion, with the distribution of fluorescence intensity across cellular regions (highlighted by the white line in Fig. 3e) appearing highly synchronized. The calculated Pearson coefficient between R-CDs and LTG amounts to 0.86, surpassing the coefficient of 0.46 obtained for the commercial green fluorescent dye targeting mitochondria (Fig. S8). This should be related to NR, the synthetic precursor of R-CDs [30]. Highly pure neutral red is a lysosomal probe that stains lysosomes with red fluorescence, and the reacted R-CDs retain both the neutrality and specificity of neutral red itself in response to lysosomes. Through colocalization experiments, it was established that R-CDs possess a cell-permeable characteristic, enabling selective localization within lysosomes and yielding accurate responses to lysosomal ATP levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time monitoring of fluctuations in endogenous lysosomal ATP levels
ce:section.ce:para: Chloroquine has been identified as a suppressor of HepG2 proliferation through its ability to diminish intracellular ATP levels [31]. Furthermore, it selectively accumulates in lysosomes and induces lysosomal migration without causing significant damage elsewhere, facilitating analyses such as ATP detection or imaging [32]. Cells were imaged at 2-min intervals after chloroquine (10 μM) was added to cells treated with R-CDs (Fig. 4 a). The resulting cells exhibited a progressive increase in fluorescence intensity in the red channel and a corresponding decrease in the yellow channel, aligning with the fluorescence changes observed in R-CDs upon reduction in ATP concentration. Consequently, the ratio of fluorescence intensity in the yellow channel to the red channel decreased over time, whereas cells unexposed with chloroquine displayed no obvious downward trend (Fig. 4b).

To further explore the sensitivity of R-CDs to endogenous ATP variations, LO2 cells pretreated with R-CDs were then stimulated with etoposide (50 μM) (Fig. 4c). Etoposide served as a crucial anticancer agent employed in the therapy of diverse cancer types [33]. Its actions include impeding nucleoside translocation and inhibiting the synthesis of DNA, RNA, and proteins, ultimately promoting ATP production [34]. Fluorescence imaging was conducted every 2 min following treatment, revealing a progressive increase in the FI ratio of R-CDs (Iyellow/Ired) over time (Fig. 4d). These results reinforce the hypothesis that R-CDs is capable of lysosomal ATP level detection in real-time.

Acetaminophen (APAP) is a widely used active ingredient in medications aimed at alleviating pain and reducing fever [35]. However, excessive APAP consumption can induce mitochondrial dysfunction, resulting in diminished ATP generation and heightened reactive oxygen species (ROS) production [36]. In this study, three pharmacological agents were employed to modulate ATP levels within cells. Cells were incubated with a glucose-free medium instead of oligomycin to inhibit ATP production. Intriguingly, as depicted in Fig. 4e–f, cells that were subjected to etoposide treatment displayed a pronounced elevation in the fluorescence intensity ratio (Iyellow/Ired) across both channels, when compared to the control LO2 cells. Conversely, cells that were treated with chloroquine, APAP, or the glucose-free medium exhibited a significant reduction in the fluorescence intensity ratio, aligning with the observed trends in R-CDs themselves in response to alterations in ATP content. These findings emphasized the potential utility of R-CDs for elucidating the impacts of drugs on endogenous cellular ATP levels and could serve as an effective tool for drug screening.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of lysosome ATP concentration in different cell lines
ce:section.ce:para: Distinguishing lysosomal ATP levels in different cell lines is crucial for understanding their role in metabolic flexibility and the maintenance of cellular homeostasis. However, conventional ATP probes with a single wavelength face limitations in distinguishing lysosomal ATP levels due to variances in cell permeability and endocytosis across different cell types. Therefore, this work aimed to investigate the efficacy of R-CDs as a tool to accurately measure lysosomal ATP levels in diverse cell types.

The experiment started with the construction of a linear calibration curve, establishing a correlation between the fluorescence intensity ratio (Iyellow/Ired) and ATP concentration (Fig. S9) [37]. This process enabled the. This calibration curve facilitated the determination of lysosomal ATP concentrations in various types of cells. In Fig. 5 , lysosomal ATP concentrations were calculated in various cell types: LO2 cells (human hepatocytes) had concentrations of 4.8 ± 0.7 mM, consistent with reported live cell values of at least 3 mM. Cancer cell lines, HeLa (human cervical cancer), HepG2 (human liver cancer), and MCF-7 (human breast cancer), exhibited higher lysosomal ATP levels, measuring 6.9 ± 0.5 mM, 7.5 ± 0.8 mM, and 6.5 ± 1.1 mM, respectively, compared to normal cells. This observation suggests that lysosomes in cancer cells require elevated ATP levels to support their malignant proliferation while maintaining lysosomal function and a heightened autophagic flux [38]. R-CDs are able to accurately differentiate between normal and cancerous cells and have the potential to diagnose cancer at the cellular level simply by fluorescence imaging.

In conclusion, the dual-emission R-CDs effectively monitored and quantified lysosomal ATP levels in live cells. The ratiometric fluorescent probe, utilizing a dynamic burst mechanism, demonstrated a reliable detection limit (1.04 μM) and a wide range (2–100 μM) with high selectivity under physiological conditions. Additionally, it showcased its applicability in ATP determination in human serum. In addition, the R-CDs in this study is biocompatible and membrane-permeable, specifically targeting lysosomes, enabling real-time detection of dynamic changes in ATP levels. It is also capable of assessing the lysosomal status of different cell lines and effectively distinguishing normal cells from cancer cells. Besides being used as lysosomal probes for ATP quantification, R-CDs are expected to be used as an effective platform for drug screening and disease diagnosis, and are hoped to become a reference tool for advancing cellular process research and deepening our understanding of drug mechanisms of action.

Jing Hu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Tianmiao Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Data curation. Jingyuan Xu: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Jun Hai: Supervision, Formal analysis. Yibing Ji: Supervision, Resources. Ruijun Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (No. 21804141).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A view of acidic intracellular compartments

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Age-related dysfunction of the autophago-lysosomal pathway in human endothelial cells
